title: The Twenty-first Sunday after Pentecost
context:
# TEMPORAL CONTEXT SNAPSHOT
Saint John's Episcopal Cathedral, Denver, Colorado
Sermon Date: October 30, 2022

## 1. LITURGICAL CONTEXT

This sermon was preached on the Twenty-first Sunday after Pentecost, placing the church in Ordinary Time following the summer season. Under the Revised Common Lectionary (RCL), the readings for this date would have been Habakkuk 1:1-4, 2:1-4; Psalm 119:137-144; 2 Thessalonians 1:1-4, 11-12; and Luke 19:1-10 (the story of Zacchaeus). The liturgical color would have been green, reflecting the continuation of the long green season of Ordinary Time stretching from Pentecost toward Advent.

## 2. EPISCOPAL / ANGLICAN CHURCH NEWS

The Episcopal Church was in the aftermath of General Convention 2022, held in Baltimore in July, where resolutions on liturgical innovation and LGBTQ+ inclusion had dominated discussion. The Diocese of Colorado was led by Bishop Kimberly Lucas (elected 2021), whose tenure was focused on reconciliation and addressing pandemic-era church decline. No major diocesan events occurred specifically the week of October 30, though parishes were preparing for All Saints' Day observances on November 1.

## 3. DENVER & COLORADO NEWS

Denver experienced typical late autumn weather in late October 2022, with temperatures declining into the 50s. The city was in the final week before the November 8 midterm elections, with gubernatorial race between Democrat Jared Polis (defending) and Republican Heidi Ganahl dominating local political coverage. The Cathedral sits in the Capitol Hill neighborhood, historically one of Denver's most progressive districts, where election canvassing and voter mobilization efforts were particularly active.

## 4. NATIONAL NEWS (US)

The dominant national story was the final week before the November 8 midterm elections, with Republicans predicting a significant red wave and Democrats mounting last-minute campaign efforts. The stock market continued its decline following months of Federal Reserve interest rate hikes, with recession fears mounting and inflation remaining elevated at around 8.2%. Hurricane Ian had made landfall in late September but recovery efforts in Florida and the Carolinas remained an active news story, with property damage estimates exceeding $100 billion.

## 5. INTERNATIONAL NEWS

Russia's invasion of Ukraine was in its ninth month, with Russian forces conducting a partial mobilization and retreating from Kherson in the south following Ukrainian counteroffensives. The UK was stabilizing under new Prime Minister Rishi Sunak (appointed October 25), following the brief Liz Truss administration and ongoing economic turmoil. China's zero-COVID lockdown policies were sparking rare public protests in major cities including Beijing and Shanghai, signaling potential fractures in Communist Party control.

## 6. CULTURAL MOMENT

American culture in late October 2022 was dominated by midterm election anxiety, economic uncertainty, and the traditional Halloween/autumn season. The Denver Broncos were struggling with a disappointing 3-4 record under new coach Nathaniel Hackett, disappointing a fan base that had expected playoff contention. The overall mood was one of national exhaustion—pandemic fatigue had not fully lifted, inflation was straining household budgets, and the political climate was increasingly polarized heading into the final days before midterm voting.
